one_line_takeaway: HuForDet unifies face-region and full-body forgery detection via a heterogeneous RGB+adaptive-LoG mixture-of-experts plus a confidence-gated MLLM branch, achieving 90.22% overall AUC on a new 1M-image benchmark by covering forgery types that no single prior detector handles well.
key_finding: On the HuFor benchmark HuForDet reaches the best overall AUC of 90.22%, beating the strongest baseline NPR (87.75%) by +2.47% and M2F2-Det (86.73%) by +3.49%, with larger gains under stringent false-positive budgets: overall TPR95 70.87% (+5.88% over NPR) and TPR99 33.45% (+9.30% over NPR). The holistic design's value is that no single prior method covers the whole spectrum — NPR actually edges HuForDet on the fully-synthetic Diff-Cele subset (95.40% vs 95.10% AUC) but collapses on partial-manipulation FF++ (82.14%), while HuForDet stays strong on both. Ablations show frequency experts matter most (Efreq alone 86.88% vs RGB alone 82.93% overall AUC), the two expert types are complementary (combined 90.42%), and — importantly — naive concatenation of the MLLM branch HURTS performance (75.75%, dropping FF++ to 69.80%) whereas the confidence-aware fusion recovers to 91.45%, a +15.70% swing that validates the confidence mechanism. Confidence scores are semantically meaningful: mean 0.80 for prompt-driven full syntheses vs 0.18 (high variance) for face-swaps where local edits are hard for the MLLM to see.
core_contribution: The paper introduces HuForDet, a holistic dual-branch framework for detecting human image forgeries that span the full spectrum from partial facial manipulations (e.g., face-swapping) to fully-synthesized full-body human images from GANs/diffusion models. Its own novel components are: (1) a Face Forgery Detection Branch (Fface) built as a mixture-of-experts (MoE) with four HETEROGENEOUS experts — two RGB spatial-domain convolutional experts (E1 with 3x3, E2 with 9x9 kernels) and two frequency-domain experts (E3, E4) based on a newly proposed adaptive Laplacian-of-Gaussian (adaLoG) block that learns per-location, multi-scale filter scales rather than using fixed sigma; (2) a Contextualized Forgery Detection Branch (Fctx) that uses an MLLM (CLIP-ViT/336px encoder + Vicuna-7B) to reason over the whole image for semantic/anatomical anomalies, and — the key novelty here — emits a self-assessed confidence score via a special <s> token whose hidden state (conditioned on CLIP global context) is regressed to c in [0,1]; and (3) a confidence-aware fusion network G that dynamically weights the contextual branch's contribution to suppress MLLM hallucination. The authors also curate HuFor, a new 1,022,217-image benchmark unifying face-forgery and full-body-synthesis data.
threat_model: Adversary: a content creator/attacker who produces forged human imagery to undermine trust in human identity/media (media-forensics setting). Attack surface is deliberately broad and unified: (a) partial manipulations that alter only facial regions while preserving image context (face-swap, attribute edit, reenactment), and (b) full-body photorealistic syntheses from GANs and personalized diffusion models (InstantID, PhotoMaker, IP-Adapter) exhibiting anatomical anomalies (extra fingers, unnatural skin, three-legged artifacts). Defender goal: a single detector that generalizes across this whole spectrum, since prior face-only detectors fail on full-body synthesis and AIGC whole-image detectors fail when only a small face region is forged. The paper frames robustness via TPR at low false-positive rates (TPR95/TPR99), reflecting deployment where false alarms are costly. No adaptive/adversarial-evasion attacker is modeled; robustness is measured as cross-forgery-type generalization, not against attacks that specifically target the detector.
limitations: (1) No adaptive-adversary / anti-forensics evaluation — 'robustness' here means cross-forgery-type generalization, not resistance to an attacker who evades the detector, nor to common perturbations (compression beyond FF++ c40, resizing, social-media laundering). (2) The Diff-Cele full-body corpus is narrow: ~30 celebrity identities x 15 activities generated by only three personalized diffusion models (InstantID, PhotoMaker, IP-Adapter), so generalization to unseen generators/identities is untested. (3) Heavy compute footprint — a Vicuna-7B MLLM plus CLIP-ViT plus a DenseNet MoE and a 3-stage pipeline; the authors explicitly flag efficiency as future work. (4) The full-body 'forgery' signal partly relies on MLLM semantic reasoning grounded in Gemini-2.0-generated rationales, inheriting whatever biases/errors that teacher model introduces. (5) Unusual data split (only 30% train, 50% test) and reliance on optimal-threshold accuracy; the abstract-level record for this paper was garbled (contained a stray reference fragment), so grounding was taken entirely from the PDF. (6) On FF++ c40 and Diff-Cele it is not the single best method (M2F2-Det / NPR respectively win those slices).

datasets_used: HuFor (newly curated, 1,022,217 images, 28+ forgery types; splits train 306,665 / val 102,222 / test 511,109)
FaceForensics++ (FF++) — partial facial manipulation videos, 1,000 originals x 4 methods, also c23/c40 compression
Diff-Cele — 317,231 fully-synthesized celebrity images from InstantID, PhotoMaker, IP-Adapter
quantitative_results: HuForDet overall on HuFor: AUC 90.22%, Acc 89.70%, TPR95 70.87%, TPR99 33.45%
HuForDet per-subset AUC: FF++ 87.80%, UniAttack+ 90.70%, Diff-Cele 95.10%
Best baseline NPR overall AUC 87.75% (TPR95 64.99%, TPR99 24.15%); HuForDet +2.47% AUC, +5.88% TPR95, +9.30% TPR99
NPR beats HuForDet on Diff-Cele (95.40% vs 95.10% AUC) but is weak on FF++ (82.14%) and UniAttack+ (86.09%)
Ablation (overall AUC): baseline DenseNet-121 82.50; +RGB experts 82.93; +freq experts 86.88; +both experts 90.42; +Fctx naive concat 75.75; +Fctx confidence fusion 91.45
adaLoG ablation (overall AUC): fixed sigma{1,4,7} 84.83, sigma{9,12,15} 84.07, both fixed 85.90, adaptive adaLoG 86.88
Face-swap benchmark (Acc/AUC): FF++ c23 99.11/99.44, FF++ c40 92.99/95.21, Celeb-DF 99.01/99.96
Fctx confidence: mean 0.80 for prompt-driven full-synthesis vs mean 0.18 for face-swap
baselines_compared: MoE-FFD (discussed as related MoE work, not a table baseline)
LLaVA-7b (qualitative attention comparison for the contextual branch)
